questions.question:
Let A be a set with n elements. Let C be a collection of distinct subsets of A such that for any two subsets S_1 and S_2 in C, either S_1 ⊆ S_2 or S_2 ⊆ S_1. What is the maximum cardinality of C?

A. n - 1
B. n + 1
C. 2^(n-1) + 1
D. n!
questions.to_find: Maximum cardinality of a chain of subsets of an n-element set

understand.plain: A collection of subsets where every pair is comparable (one contains the other) is called a chain in the power set lattice. The longest chain in 2^A goes from the empty set through all singletons, pairs, triples, ..., all the way up to A itself — one set of each size from 0 to n. That gives n + 1 subsets.

keywords.explain: A collection of sets where any two are related by inclusion (every pair is comparable).
The set of all subsets of A ordered by inclusion; it forms a lattice where joins are unions and meets are intersections.
The length of the longest chain in 2^A equals n + 1, corresponding to one subset of each possible cardinality 0, 1, 2, ..., n.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 360 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='360' height='220' rx='12' class='bg'/><text x='180' y='24' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Longest chain in 2^A, |A|=3</text><line x1='180' y1='185' x2='180' y2='165' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='180' y1='145' x2='180' y2='125' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='180' y1='105' x2='180' y2='85' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><ellipse cx='180' cy='195' rx='50' ry='16' class='hi'/><text x='180' y='199' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>{1,2,3}</text><ellipse cx='180' cy='155' rx='42' ry='14' class='bg'/><text x='180' y='159' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>{1,2}</text><ellipse cx='180' cy='115' rx='34' ry='14' class='bg'/><text x='180' y='119' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>{1}</text><ellipse cx='180' cy='75' rx='28' ry='14' class='bg'/><text x='180' y='79' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>{}</text><text x='310' y='199' class='sub mut'>n+1 = 4</text><text x='310' y='160' class='sub mut'>nodes</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A chain of 4 subsets: empty set, {1}, {1,2}, {1,2,3}, each contained in the next, showing the maximum chain length of n+1 for a 3-element set.

given.aim: Find the maximum number of subsets of A that can form a chain under inclusion.

terms.connects: Each subset of A is a node in 2^A.

terms.meaning: A collection of distinct subsets of A where every pair is comparable by inclusion.
terms.connects: C is a chain in the power set lattice of A.

terms.meaning: The number of sets in C.
terms.connects: We want to maximize this length.

given.plan: Build a chain by picking exactly one subset of each size: the empty set (size 0), then one singleton (size 1), one 2-element subset, ..., up to A itself (size n). This produces n+1 sets, and no longer chain exists since all (n+2) cardinality values 0..n are already used.

steps.title: Construct the maximum chain
steps.apply: List subsets of A by including exactly one of each size: {}, {a_1}, {a_1, a_2}, ..., {a_1, a_2, ..., a_n} = A. Each consecutive pair satisfies the inclusion condition, giving a valid chain of n + 1 subsets.
steps.note: Any chain can contain at most one subset of each size (0, 1, 2, ..., n), since two subsets of the same size are comparable only if they are equal.

steps.title: Show n+1 is the maximum
steps.apply: A chain in 2^A has strictly increasing sizes at each step. The sizes must be a strictly increasing subsequence of {0, 1, 2, ..., n}, which has n+1 elements. So any chain has at most n+1 elements.
steps.note: The chain {} ⊆ {a_1} ⊆ ... ⊆ A achieves this bound, confirming the maximum is exactly n+1.

solution.result: The maximum cardinality of C is n + 1.

questions.formula_note: The key insight is that a chain in 2^A has at most one element per subset-size level (0 through n), giving at most n+1 elements; the straight-line chain {} ⊆ {a_1} ⊆ ... ⊆ A achieves this bound.

questions.question:
Let P, Q, and R be sets. Let delta denote the symmetric difference operator defined as P delta Q = (P ∪ Q) - (P ∩ Q). Using Venn diagrams, determine which of the following is/are TRUE?

I. P delta (Q ∩ R) = (P delta Q) ∩ (P delta R)
II. P delta (Q ∪ R) = (P delta Q) ∪ (P delta R)

A. I only
B. II only
C. Neither I nor II
D. Both I and II
questions.to_find: Which of the two symmetric difference distributivity laws hold

understand.plain: The symmetric difference P△Q contains elements in exactly one of P or Q. The question asks whether symmetric difference distributes over intersection (I) and over union (II). Both statements are false — symmetric difference does NOT distribute over either ∩ or ∪ in general. A simple counterexample with concrete sets disproves each.

keywords.explain: P△Q = (P ∪ Q) − (P ∩ Q), the set of elements in exactly one of P or Q.
P△(Q∩R) = (P△Q)∩(P△R) is the claimed law I; it is FALSE in general.
P△(Q∪R) = (P△Q)∪(P△R) is claimed in II; it is also FALSE in general.
keywords.example: P = {1}, Q = {1,2}, R = {1,3}: LHS = P△{1,2,3} = {2,3}, RHS = {2}∪{3} = {2,3} — happens to agree, but other cases fail.

understand.visual_alt: Three overlapping circles for sets P, Q, R illustrating that P△(Q∩R) and (P△Q)∩(P△R) shade different regions, disproving both distributivity laws.

given.aim: Determine which distributivity identities for symmetric difference over intersection and union are valid.

terms.meaning: Symmetric difference: elements in exactly one of P or Q.
Intersection: elements in both Q and R.
terms.connects: Used on the right side of the delta operator in statement I.

terms.meaning: Union: elements in Q or R or both.
terms.connects: Used on the right side of the delta operator in statement II.

terms.meaning: A specific assignment of sets that makes a purported identity false.
terms.example: P = {1,2}, Q = {1,3}, R = {1,4} disproves statement I.
terms.connects: One valid counterexample is enough to show the general law fails.

given.plan: Try a small concrete example for each statement. If LHS != RHS for the chosen sets, the statement is false.

steps.title: Test Statement I with a counterexample
steps.formula_raw: P delta (Q ∩ R) vs (P delta Q) ∩ (P delta R)
steps.note: Symmetric difference does not distribute over intersection in general.

steps.title: Test Statement II with a counterexample
steps.formula_raw: P delta (Q ∪ R) vs (P delta Q) ∪ (P delta R)
steps.note: Symmetric difference does not distribute over union in general either.

solution.result: Neither Statement I nor Statement II is true in general. Answer: C.

questions.formula_note: The symmetric difference operator is associative and commutative and forms a group under addition modulo 2 on subsets, but it does NOT distribute over either ∩ or ∪; counterexamples disprove both I and II.

questions.question:
What is the cardinality of the set of integers defined below?

X = {n | 1 <= n <= 123, n is not divisible by either 2, 3, or 5}

A. 30
B. 33
C. 37
D. 44
questions.to_find: Count of integers from 1 to 123 not divisible by 2, 3, or 5

understand.plain: We need to count integers from 1 to 123 that are coprime to 30 (not divisible by 2, 3, or 5). The standard approach is inclusion-exclusion: count how many are divisible by at least one of 2, 3, 5, then subtract from 123. The numbers divisible by exactly none of them is |X|.

keywords.explain: |A ∪ B ∪ C| = |A| + |B| + |C| - |A∩B| - |A∩C| - |B∩C| + |A∩B∩C|, used to count elements divisible by at least one prime.
Number of multiples of k in {1, ..., N} is floor(N/k).
Count those NOT in the excluded set: |X| = N - |A∪B∪C|.

understand.visual_alt: Three overlapping circles for multiples of 2, 3, and 5 within {1,...,123}, with the region outside all three representing the 33 integers coprime to 30.

given.aim: Count integers n with 1 <= n <= 123 that are not divisible by 2, 3, or 5.

terms.meaning: The upper bound of the range, N = 123.
terms.connects: Sets the range for floor-division counting.

terms.meaning: Sets of multiples of 2, 3, and 5 respectively in {1,...,123}.
terms.connects: We apply inclusion-exclusion on A ∪ B ∪ C.

terms.meaning: Number of integers in {1,...,N} divisible by k.
terms.connects: Used for each single prime and each pairwise/triple product.

given.plan: Apply inclusion-exclusion to count multiples of 2, 3, or 5 in {1,...,123}, then subtract from 123 to get the count of numbers coprime to 30.

steps.title: Count multiples of each prime and their LCMs
Apply inclusion-exclusion and take complement
steps.note: Alternatively, the density of numbers coprime to 30 in a full period of 30 is phi(30)/30 = 8/30. Over 4 complete periods (120 integers), that is 4*8 = 32, plus checking 121, 122, 123 manually: none are coprime to 30 (122=2*61, 123=3*41), giving 32+1=33 (121=11^2 is coprime).

questions.formula_note: Inclusion-exclusion on the three divisibility sets (multiples of 2, 3, 5 and their LCM pairings) counts the 90 excluded integers; the remaining 33 form the answer.
